title: Bearing With — Sati & Khanti
description: Mindfulness as the capacity to remain present to what is happening, and endurance as the willingness to stay when the mind wants to leave. The twin faculties that sustain the practitioner through the long middle of the path.

sequences.title: What Mindfulness Is Not
sequences.description: Clearing away the modern misunderstandings so that the original faculty can come into view.

content.body:
The word *sati* has suffered more in translation than almost any other term in the Buddhist vocabulary. In the modern West, it has become *mindfulness*, and mindfulness has become an industry, and the industry has reshaped the word until it no longer points at what the suttas meant by it.

The modern understanding of mindfulness is, roughly: paying close attention to what is happening in the present moment, without judgment. This is the definition taught in clinical settings, in workplace wellness programs, in apps that guide you through ten minutes of noticing your breath. It is not wrong in the way that a lie is wrong. It is wrong in the way that a photograph of a landscape is wrong — it captures something real, but it flattens the depth, removes the weather, and makes the thing portable in a way the original never was.

Sati in the suttas is not primarily about paying attention. It is about remembering. The root of the word is *sarati* — to remember, to recollect, to keep in mind. What is being remembered is not the present moment, which does not need to be remembered because it is already here. What is being remembered is the context in which the present moment is occurring. The practitioner with sati is not simply aware of what is happening. They are aware of what is happening in light of what they know about the nature of what is happening.

The difference is significant. A person without sati can be highly attentive — can notice the breath with great precision, can track sensations with acute sensitivity — and still be completely lost. They are lost because they are attending without context. They are watching the breath but have forgotten why they are watching the breath. They are tracking sensations but have lost the framework within which the tracking is meaningful. The attention is present. The understanding that gives the attention its direction is absent.

Sati restores the framework. It is the faculty that keeps the practitioner oriented — that holds in peripheral awareness the reasons for the practice, the dangers of being swept away by feeling, the tendency of the mind to reach and flinch, the impermanent and unsatisfactory nature of what arises. It does not hold all of this in focused attention. It holds it the way an experienced driver holds awareness of the road while thinking about other things — not concentrating on it, not ignoring it, but maintaining a background orientation that shapes behavior without requiring explicit engagement.

This is why sati is placed after indriyasaṃvara in the gradual training rather than before it. Sense restraint develops the capacity to stand at the doors without being dragged through them. Sati provides the context that makes the standing meaningful. Without sati, sense restraint is willpower — holding back against a pull without understanding why one is holding back. With sati, sense restraint is informed — the practitioner holds back because they remember, in a way deeper than conceptual, what following the pull leads to.

The modern reduction of sati to bare attention has produced a generation of practitioners who are very good at noticing and not very good at understanding what they notice. They can sit for an hour and track the breath. They cannot explain, from their own direct knowledge, why tracking the breath matters. The attention has been trained. The wisdom that should accompany the attention has not. And attention without wisdom is, in the tradition's frank assessment, not sati at all. It is just paying attention.

content.prompt: Consider your own relationship to the word mindfulness and whatever practices you associate with it. Have you experienced the distinction described above — between bare attention and contextual awareness? Can you recall a moment of practice in which you were paying attention carefully but had lost the thread of why you were paying attention? Describe what that felt like, and what, if anything, brought the context back.
content.sampleAnswer: The distinction is often invisible until it is named, at which point many practitioners recognize it immediately. The experience of meditating with great attentional clarity but having lost all sense of why is remarkably common and rarely discussed, because the attentional clarity feels like success. The practitioner is sitting still, focused, present — all the things they were told to be. But the practice has become mechanical. The attention is running on its own, disconnected from any understanding that might give it direction. What typically restores the context is not more attention but a shift in register — a moment of stepping back, of recalling what the practice is for, of reconnecting the activity with its purpose. That shift is sati.

sequences.title: Remembering the Context
sequences.description: What sati holds, and how it holds it.

content.body:
If sati is remembering, the question is: remembering what?

The suttas are specific. The practitioner with sati remembers the Dhamma — the teaching. Not as a set of propositions to be recited, but as a lens through which present experience is understood. The practitioner remembers that what arises will pass. That pleasant feeling, if grasped, leads to suffering. That the self who seems to be having the experience is not what it appears to be. That the mind's automatic responses are conditioned and not necessary. That there is a way through, and that it requires a specific kind of work.

This is not an intellectual exercise. The practitioner is not sitting there thinking about impermanence while they watch the breath. The remembering is more subtle than that — it is a coloring of awareness, a quality of being oriented, that shapes how experience is received without requiring explicit thought. A practitioner with well-developed sati receives a pleasant sensation and, without having to think about it, does not grasp. They do not grasp not because they are exerting willpower, but because they are situated, by sati, in a context in which the grasping is recognized as unnecessary. The context does the work. The practitioner's job is to maintain the context.

The four foundations of mindfulness — *satipaṭṭhāna* — describe the domains in which sati operates. Body, feeling, mind, and dhammas. The practitioner maintains awareness of the body as body — not as my body, not as a beautiful or ugly body, but as a process of physical phenomena arising and passing. Of feelings as feelings — not as my feelings, not as evidence about the world, but as feeling tones that arise from contact and condition response. Of the mind as mind — not as my mind, not as a fixed entity, but as a stream of mental states, each one arising from conditions and passing when those conditions change. And of dhammas as dhammas — the categories and structures of the teaching as they appear in direct experience.

The word *foundation* is significant. These are not four objects to be contemplated in sequence. They are four ways of being grounded. The practitioner who is established in satipaṭṭhāna is grounded in a way of receiving experience that is informed by understanding. The body is not denied or transcended. It is understood as what it is. Feelings are not suppressed or indulged. They are understood as what they are. The mind is not controlled or emptied. It is understood as what it is.

This grounding is what prevents the practice from becoming mere technique. A practitioner without sati can follow the instructions — can watch the breath, can scan the body, can note the arising and passing of sensations — but the following is hollow. It produces results of a kind: some calm, some concentration, some familiarity with the mechanics of attention. But it does not produce understanding, because understanding requires that the practitioner know what they are looking at and why it matters. Sati provides the knowing.

The practitioner is not expected to have this knowing fully formed at the beginning. Sati develops over time, as the understanding it carries deepens through study, through practice, and through direct seeing. The beginning practitioner's sati is thin — it holds a rough outline of the teaching, an approximate sense of direction, a general memory that the mind's automatic responses are not to be trusted. The advanced practitioner's sati is thick — it holds a detailed, experientially verified understanding that colors every moment of perception. The path from thin to thick is the path itself.

content.prompt: What do you actually carry with you, in terms of understanding, that shapes how you receive experience? Not what you believe intellectually. What you know from your own observation — about impermanence, about the mind's tendencies, about the relationship between feeling and response. Try to distinguish between what you have read about and what you have seen for yourself. The distinction matters.
content.sampleAnswer: The distinction between what has been read and what has been seen is the central diagnostic of this prompt. Most practitioners, when honest, find that their understanding is largely conceptual — they know that things are impermanent because they have read that things are impermanent, not because they have watched impermanence operating in their own experience with enough sustained attention to have it change how they receive the next sensation. The conceptual understanding is not worthless. It provides the initial framework. But it is not sati. Sati is what develops when the conceptual understanding has been verified, even partially, by direct observation, and the verification begins to operate below the level of conscious thought.

sequences.title: Endurance
sequences.description: What khanti is, and why the tradition pairs it with sati rather than with effort.

content.body:
The Pāli word *khanti* is usually translated as patience. The translation is not wrong, but it understates the quality. Patience, in ordinary English, implies waiting — enduring a delay, tolerating an inconvenience, putting up with something until it changes. Khanti is more than this. Khanti is the willingness to remain with what is difficult without requiring it to change.

The distinction matters because it marks the difference between two fundamentally different orientations toward discomfort. The patient person endures because they believe the discomfort will end. The practitioner with khanti endures without that belief. They are willing to stay with what is unpleasant, what is boring, what is painful, what is frightening, not because they expect it to resolve but because they have understood that the impulse to flee is itself the problem.

This is the quality the tradition pairs with sati, and the pairing is not accidental. Sati provides the context — the understanding that what is arising is conditioned, impermanent, and not-self. Khanti provides the staying power — the capacity to remain present to what sati is illuminating, even when what is illuminated is unwelcome. Without sati, khanti is mere stubbornness — gritting one's teeth against discomfort with no understanding of why. Without khanti, sati collapses at the first sign of difficulty — the practitioner sees clearly for a moment, finds what they see unpleasant, and retreats.

The retreat is the default. The mind's fundamental response to unpleasant experience is to leave — to distract, to suppress, to reframe, to fix, to do anything other than remain with the unpleasantness as it is. This response is not a character flaw. It is the deep structure of a mind that has been optimizing for comfort since before it could form a sentence. The response runs beneath the level of decision. By the time the practitioner notices they have left, they have already been gone for some time.

Khanti is the faculty that allows the practitioner to notice the impulse to leave and to stay anyway. Not to prevent the impulse. Not to condemn it. Simply to not follow it. The impulse arises — the discomfort is too much, the boredom is unbearable, the sensation is too intense, the thought is too painful — and instead of acting on the impulse, the practitioner remains. They feel the impulse to flee. They feel the discomfort the impulse is trying to escape. And they do not move.

This is not heroic. It is not dramatic. From the outside, nothing is happening. A person is sitting still. From the inside, something demanding is happening: the mind is being asked to tolerate what it has never been required to tolerate. Not extreme pain — ordinary discomfort. The ache in the knee. The itch on the nose. The restlessness that has no object. The boredom that feels, in the moment, like it might never end. These are small things. They feel, while they are being endured without escape, enormous.

The tradition is frank about this. The suttas describe the practitioner as one who endures cold and heat, hunger and thirst, the touch of flies and mosquitoes, wind and sun and creeping things, abusive and hurtful language, and bodily feelings that are painful, sharp, piercing, disagreeable, distressing, and threatening to life. This is not hyperbole. It is the tradition's description of what practice actually requires — not once, not in a retreat, but as an ongoing feature of a life devoted to the work.

The purpose of khanti is not suffering. The purpose is access. Certain things about the mind's operations can only be seen when the mind is not fleeing. The pattern of reaching and flinching — the automatic response to pleasant and unpleasant — is invisible when the flinching is allowed to proceed, because the flinching removes the practitioner from the experience before the experience can be fully seen. Khanti keeps the practitioner present long enough to see what the flinching was hiding. And what it was hiding, in most cases, is surprisingly simple: that the discomfort was not as bad as the mind was claiming, and that the mind's urgency to flee was out of proportion to the actual threat.

content.prompt: Recall a time — in practice or in daily life — when you stayed with something uncomfortable longer than the mind wanted you to. Not something extreme. Something small. An awkward silence you did not fill. A physical discomfort you did not immediately adjust. A difficult emotion you did not distract yourself from. What happened when you stayed? Not what you thought about it. What happened.
content.sampleAnswer: The most common report from this exercise is anticlimactic, and the anticlimax is the point. When the practitioner stays with a small discomfort rather than fleeing, what usually happens is: the discomfort changes. Not always by diminishing — sometimes it intensifies, sometimes it shifts, sometimes it simply becomes different. But it changes. The mind had predicted, implicitly, that the discomfort would be permanent and intolerable if not escaped. The prediction was wrong. The willingness to test the prediction — to stay long enough to see that it was wrong — is khanti. And the discovery that the prediction was wrong is one of the first reliable fruits of the practice.

sequences.title: The Long Middle
sequences.description: What practice actually looks like when the initial enthusiasm has passed and the results have not yet come.

content.body:
Every practitioner who stays with the path long enough will enter a period that does not have a name in the tradition but that the tradition clearly knows about. It is the period after the initial enthusiasm and before any deep change. It is the long middle.

In the beginning, the practice has the quality of discovery. Everything is new. The teachings are fresh. The first experiences of sitting still, of noticing the mind's operations, of catching a reflex before it fires — these feel significant, even revelatory. There is energy. There is motivation. The practitioner reads voraciously, sits with dedication, and feels that they are on the way to something.

This period ends. It does not end because the practitioner fails. It ends because the novelty runs out. The teachings are no longer fresh; they are familiar. The experiences of sitting still are no longer revelatory; they are repetitive. The catching of reflexes, which once felt like a breakthrough, now feels like basic maintenance. The practitioner knows what they are supposed to do. They have been doing it for months, perhaps years. And they cannot honestly say that they are fundamentally different from when they started.

This is the long middle, and it is where most practitioners quit. They do not quit dramatically. They quit gradually, in the way described in the first course of this series — by erosion, by exception, by the slow reassertion of the old allegiances. The practice session gets shorter. The reading falls off. The precepts, held carefully for a time, begin to flex. The practitioner does not announce that they have given up. They simply stop showing up, and the life they had been simplifying begins to refill.

The tradition addresses this period not with encouragement but with a sober description of what is happening. What is happening is that the practitioner has arrived at the place where the practice becomes work rather than discovery. The initial fuel — curiosity, novelty, the pleasure of new understanding — has been consumed. The practice now runs on something else, and the something else has not yet been established. The practitioner is between fuels. The old motivation is gone and the new motivation — which comes from direct seeing, from verified understanding, from the kind of knowing that sati carries — has not yet matured.

Sati and khanti together are what carry the practitioner through this interval. Sati, because it holds the memory of why the work matters even when nothing in the present moment is confirming it. Khanti, because it allows the practitioner to remain with the flatness, the boredom, the sense of not getting anywhere, without converting that discomfort into a reason to stop.

The tradition does not promise that the long middle will end quickly. It does promise that it will end. The practitioner who stays — who continues to sit, to study, to keep the precepts, to maintain sense restraint, through the period in which none of it seems to be producing results — will eventually arrive at a different relationship to the practice. The practice will stop being something they do and will start being something they are. The effort will become less effortful. The maintenance will become less burdensome. Not because the practitioner has achieved anything, but because the faculties of sati and khanti, exercised over time, have begun to function on their own.

This transition is not dramatic. There is no moment of breakthrough. There is simply a gradual recognition, emerging over months, that the practice is no longer being sustained by willpower. It is being sustained by something quieter and more durable — a steadiness that was not there before and that did not arrive through any particular experience. It arrived through the accumulation of days in which the practitioner showed up and did the work when there was no reason to show up other than the work itself.

content.prompt: Are you in the long middle? Or have you been there and left — either by pushing through or by quietly stopping? Describe where you are in relation to the pattern described above, without flattering or condemning the position. If you have stopped a practice that once mattered to you, try to identify the point at which the stopping began. Not the moment you decided to stop. The earlier moment, when the fuel changed and nothing replaced it.
content.sampleAnswer: The value of this prompt is in its refusal to treat any position as wrong. The practitioner who is in the long middle and knows it is in a stronger position than the practitioner who has left without noticing. The practitioner who has stopped is not failed — they are informed, if they choose to be, about what their practice needed and did not have. In most cases, what was missing was not effort or sincerity but the twin faculties this course describes: the contextual awareness that makes the practice meaningful beyond novelty, and the willingness to stay when the meaning is not being confirmed by experience. Identifying the moment the fuel changed — the transition from discovery to maintenance — is the beginning of understanding what the next fuel needs to be.

sequences.title: Presence Without Method
sequences.description: What sati and khanti produce when they are working together, and how it differs from technique.

content.body:
A practitioner who has cultivated sati and khanti over time begins to exhibit a quality that the tradition recognizes but that modern meditation culture has largely lost track of. It is a quality of being present without doing anything in particular to be present.

Modern practice culture is heavily technique-oriented. The practitioner learns a method — breath counting, body scanning, noting, labeling, open awareness, loving-kindness — and applies it during a designated practice period. The method structures the attention. The attention, so structured, produces certain experiences. The experiences are evaluated against a framework of expected results. Progress is measured by the quality and consistency of the experiences.

This approach is not useless. Techniques produce real effects. But the effects are, in an important sense, the effects of the technique rather than the effects of the practitioner's development. The calm that arises from breath counting depends on the breath counting. Remove the technique, and the calm does not persist. The practitioner has learned to produce a state. They have not developed a faculty.

Sati and khanti, cultivated over time, produce something different. They produce a way of being in the world that does not depend on any particular method being applied. The practitioner is not doing a technique. They are carrying, as a baseline quality of awareness, the contextual understanding that sati provides and the willingness to remain that khanti provides. This baseline is present while they work, while they walk, while they talk, while they do nothing. It is not a state that is entered and exited. It is a faculty that has been developed and that now operates as part of the practitioner's ordinary equipment.

The Hillside Hermitage lineage has been particularly insistent on this distinction, and the insistence is worth attending to. The distinction is between *samatha* — calm — and *vipassanā* — insight. Both are part of the path. But in much of modern practice, samatha has been treated as the main event, and vipassanā has been reduced to a technique applied within the frame of samatha. The practitioner calms the mind through concentration and then applies insight technique to the calm mind. The calm is the container. The insight is what happens inside the container.

The gradual training reverses this. Sati and khanti are faculties of insight, not of calm. They produce understanding, not tranquility. The practitioner who has developed them is not necessarily calm. They may be agitated, tired, distracted, uncomfortable. But they are present to their agitation in a way that the practitioner who relies on technique is not. They can see the agitation as a conditioned phenomenon arising from identifiable causes. They can endure the agitation without fleeing into distraction or technique. They are, in the tradition's precise phrase, *yathābhūtañāṇadassana* — seeing things as they actually are, not as the mind, left to its own devices, would represent them.

This seeing is not a state of extraordinary perception. It is the ordinary perception of a mind that is not distorting. Most minds distort continuously — adding pleasant coloring to what is pleasant, adding threatening coloring to what is unpleasant, ignoring what is neutral. The mind with developed sati does not add the coloring. It sees what arrives as what arrives. The mind with developed khanti does not flinch from what it sees. It stays. And the combination of not-distorting and not-flinching is what the tradition means by right mindfulness — *sammā sati*.

The practitioner does not achieve this as a permanent state. They achieve it as an increasingly available capacity. Moments of undistorted seeing become more frequent, longer, and less dependent on ideal conditions. The seeing begins to operate in daily life, not only in formal practice. And the seeing, once it is operating, changes everything — not through any spectacular experience, but through the cumulative effect of seeing what is actually there rather than what the mind would prefer to be there.

content.prompt: Have you experienced the difference between a calm that depends on technique and an awareness that does not? It may have been brief — a moment of walking or sitting in which you were not doing any particular practice but were nonetheless present in a way that felt different from ordinary distraction. If you can recall such a moment, describe what was different about it. If you cannot, describe what you imagine the difference might be, and notice where your imagination may be borrowing from ideas rather than from experience.
content.sampleAnswer: This prompt is designed to surface the difference between the two kinds of presence, and to help the practitioner notice which one they are more familiar with. Many practitioners find that their experience of presence is almost entirely technique-dependent — that they feel present during practice and not-present otherwise. Others recall brief moments of a different quality, usually in unexpected contexts: a moment of clarity during an ordinary task, a sudden presence that arrived without being cultivated. These moments are not achievements. They are glimpses of the faculty that sati and khanti, developed over time, make available as a baseline rather than as an exception.

sequences.title: What Carries You
sequences.description: The relationship between sati, khanti, and what comes next in the training.

content.body:
The practitioner who has worked with sati and khanti through the long middle, and who has begun to develop the baseline quality of informed presence described in the last movement, stands now at the threshold of what the tradition considers the deeper territory of the path. What comes next — withdrawal, jhāna, and ultimately the insight that sees things as they are — requires the faculties that sati and khanti have been developing. Without them, the deeper territory is inaccessible. With them, the deeper territory is not guaranteed, but the practitioner is equipped to enter it.

The image the tradition uses is of a person crossing a river. The far bank is the goal. The river is the practice. Sīla is the decision not to carry unnecessary weight into the river. Indriyasaṃvara is the discipline of not reaching for floating objects as they pass. Sati is the knowledge of the direction — the far bank, held in awareness even when the current is strong and the bank is not visible. And khanti is the willingness to keep swimming when the swimming is hard and the bank seems no closer than it was.

None of these, individually, gets the practitioner across. Together, they create the conditions under which crossing becomes possible. The crossing itself — what happens in the deep water — is the subject of the next two courses. Withdrawal from sensory preoccupation, the arising of jhāna, and the insight that comes when the mind, for the first time, sees its own operations without distortion.

But there is something worth saying before that territory is entered, and it is this: the faculties developed in the stages covered so far — pabbajjā, sīla, indriyasaṃvara, sati, khanti — are not stages that are completed and left behind. They are not rooms that are walked through on the way to a destination. They are capacities that are maintained throughout the path and that continue to operate even after deeper experiences have arisen.

The advanced practitioner still practices pabbajjā — still maintains the internal simplification, still resists the re-accumulation of unnecessary complexity. Still practices sīla — still keeps the precepts, still attends to the weight that misconduct generates. Still practices indriyasaṃvara — still guards the sense doors, still notices the seam between feeling and response. Still practices sati — still holds the context, still remembers the teaching, still sees what arises in light of what is understood. Still practices khanti — still remains with what is difficult, still does not flee when the mind would prefer to flee.

These are not preliminary practices. They are the practice. The deeper experiences that arise in the later stages of the training arise within the container that these faculties provide, and they cannot be sustained without the container. A practitioner who enters jhāna but has abandoned sīla will find the jhāna unstable. A practitioner who sees impermanence clearly but has lost the thread of sati will find the seeing intermittent. The faculties support each other and are all maintained, in varying degrees of emphasis, throughout.

What sati and khanti specifically contribute to what comes next is this: they allow the practitioner to remain present during experiences that the untrained mind cannot tolerate. The withdrawal from sensory preoccupation produces a period of discomfort — a kind of sensory hunger — that most people reflexively relieve. Khanti allows the practitioner to stay with the hunger. Sati provides the understanding that the hunger is conditioned and will pass. Together, they hold the practitioner in the space where jhāna, if it is going to arise, arises.

And they hold the practitioner afterward, too — in the ordinary life that continues between periods of practice, in the long days that contain no special experiences, in the sustained, undramatic work of maintaining a mind that is simpler than it was. This maintenance is not glamorous. It is not the part of the path that anyone writes books about. But it is what carries the practitioner, day by day, across the river they have entered.

content.prompt: Of the faculties covered so far in this series — the internal simplification (pabbajjā), the restraint of conduct (sīla), the guarding of the senses (indriyasaṃvara), the contextual awareness (sati), and the willingness to endure (khanti) — which one do you think is most developed in you, and which one is weakest? Be specific about why. Not which one you most admire or most want to develop. Which one, based on how you actually live, is most present and which is most absent.
content.sampleAnswer: The value of this assessment is in its honesty rather than its accuracy. The practitioner does not need to be right about which faculty is strongest. They need to be willing to look. Most people find that the faculties are unevenly developed in ways they had not noticed — that they have, for instance, a natural khanti but weak sati, or strong sati but almost no indriyasaṃvara. The unevenness is normal and informative. It tells the practitioner where the path is most available to them and where it will require the most deliberate work. A balanced development of all five faculties is the aim, but the starting point is always uneven, and knowing the shape of the unevenness is the first step toward addressing it.
